title: एनसीआर में पूरे सप्ताह रहेगा बादलों का डेरा: दिल्ली-NCR में हुई बूंदाबांदी; भीषण गर्मी और उमस से मिली राहत - Amar Ujala
search_term: (लू OR "लू चलना" OR "भीषण गर्मी" OR "प्रचंड गर्मी" OR "कड़ी धूप" OR "तेज धूप" OR "गर्म हवा" OR "उष्ण लहर" OR "ताप लहर" OR "हीट वेव" OR "झुलसाने वाली गर्मी" OR "तपती गर्मी" OR "भयंकर गर्मी" OR "गर्मी का कहर" OR "गर्मी का प्रकोप" OR "अत्यधिक गर्मी" OR मौसम OR "मौसम अपडेट" OR "मौसम का हाल" OR "मौसम पूर्वानुमान" OR "मौसम अलर्ट" OR आईएमडी OR "आईएमडी पूर्वानुमान" OR "आईएमडी अलर्ट" OR "तापमान में बढ़ोतरी" OR "गर्मी से राहत" OR "गर्मी से निजात") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
एनसीआर में पूरे सप्ताह रहेगा बादलों का डेरा: दिल्ली-NCR में हुई बूंदाबांदी; भीषण गर्मी और उमस से मिली राहत
राष्ट्रीय राजधानी क्षेत्र में शुक्रवार सुबह बूंदाबांदी हुई, जिससे गर्मी और उमस से राहत मिली। मौसम विभाग ने पूरे सप्ताह हल्की बारिश की संभावना जताई है। इससे तापमान नियंत्रित रहेगा।
खबरें लगातार पढ़ने के लिए अमर उजाला एप डाउनलोड करें
या
वेबसाइट पर पढ़ना जारी रखने के लिए वीडियो विज्ञापन देखें
अगर आपके पास प्रीमियम मेंबरशिप है तो
विस्तार
दिल्ली और राष्ट्रीय राजधानी क्षेत्र (एनसीआर) में शुक्रवार सुबह बूंदाबांदी हुई, जिससे मौसम सुहावना हो गया है। बारिश के चलते गर्मी और उमस की मार झेल रहे लोगों को राहत मिली है। आने वाले दिनों में भी मौसम सुहावना रहेगा। भारत मौसम विज्ञान विभाग (आईएमडी) के पूर्वानुमान के अनुसार, पूरे सप्ताह आसमान पर बादलों का डेरा रहेगा। हल्की बारिश या बूंदाबांदी के आसार हैं, जिससे गर्मी और उमस से राहत मिलेगी।
आईएमडी ने 24 जुलाई को अधिकतम तापमान 35 डिग्री और न्यूनतम 25 डिग्री सेल्सियस रहने का अनुमान जताया है। 29 जुलाई तक अधिकतम तापमान 34 डिग्री सेल्सियस तक गिर सकता है। मानसूनी गतिविधियां कमजोर होने से तेज बारिश की संभावना कम है। बादलों की मौजूदगी और हल्की फुहारों से तापमान नियंत्रित रहेगा।
कल बृहस्पतिवार को दिनभर बादल और सूरज के बीच लुकाछिपी का खेल जारी रहा। लेकिन, इस दौरान लोगों को उमस भरी गर्मी से राहत नहीं मिली। मानसून के सक्रिय होने की उम्मीद के बीच दिल्लीवासियों को बारिश का इंतजार रहा, लेकिन गर्मी ने लोगों की सारी उम्मीदों पर पानी फेर दिया।
पढ़ें पूर्वानुमान
दिल्ली में अगले छह दिनों तक मौसम में ज्यादा बदलाव की संभावना नहीं है। मौसम विभाग के अनुसार 24, 25, 27 और 28 जुलाई को आसमान में बादल छाए रहेंगे और कई इलाकों में बहुत हल्की से हल्की बारिश हो सकती है। ऐसे में 24 जुलाई को सुबह से दोपहर के बीच 20 से 30 किमी प्रति घंटे की रफ्तार से तेज हवाएं चलने की भी संभावना है।
वहीं, 26 और 29 जुलाई को बारिश के आसार नहीं हैं, हालांकि पूरे दिन बादल छाए रह सकते हैं। इस दौरान अधिकतम तापमान 33 से 38 डिग्री सेल्सियस और न्यूनतम तापमान 23 से 27 डिग्री सेल्सियस के बीच रहने का अनुमान है।

title: एनसीआर में पूरे सप्ताह बादलों का डेरा, हल्की बारिश के आसार; भीषण गर्मी और उमस से मिलेगी राहत - bhaskarhindi.com
search_term: (लू OR "लू चलना" OR "भीषण गर्मी" OR "प्रचंड गर्मी" OR "कड़ी धूप" OR "तेज धूप" OR "गर्म हवा" OR "उष्ण लहर" OR "ताप लहर" OR "हीट वेव" OR "झुलसाने वाली गर्मी" OR "तपती गर्मी" OR "भयंकर गर्मी" OR "गर्मी का कहर" OR "गर्मी का प्रकोप" OR "अत्यधिक गर्मी" OR मौसम OR "मौसम अपडेट" OR "मौसम का हाल" OR "मौसम पूर्वानुमान" OR "मौसम अलर्ट" OR आईएमडी OR "आईएमडी पूर्वानुमान" OR "आईएमडी अलर्ट" OR "तापमान में बढ़ोतरी" OR "गर्मी से राहत" OR "गर्मी से निजात") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
एनसीआर में पूरे सप्ताह बादलों का डेरा, हल्की बारिश के आसार; भीषण गर्मी और उमस से मिलेगी राहत
By - |24 July 2026 10:05 AM IST
राष्ट्रीय राजधानी क्षेत्र (एनसीआर) में आने वाले दिनों में मौसम सुहावना बना रहने की संभावना है। भारत मौसम विज्ञान विभाग (आईएमडी) के ताजा पूर्वानुमान के अनुसार पूरे सप्ताह आसमान पर बादलों का डेरा रहेगा। हालांकि कहीं-कहीं हल्की बारिश या बूंदाबांदी होने की संभावना जताई गई है, लेकिन व्यापक या तेज बारिश के आसार फिलहाल कम हैं। लेकिन भीषण गर्मी और उमस से इस पूरे सप्ताह काफी राहत मिलने की उम्मीद है।
नोएडा, 24 जुलाई (आईएएनएस)। राष्ट्रीय राजधानी क्षेत्र (एनसीआर) में आने वाले दिनों में मौसम सुहावना बना रहने की संभावना है। भारत मौसम विज्ञान विभाग (आईएमडी) के ताजा पूर्वानुमान के अनुसार पूरे सप्ताह आसमान पर बादलों का डेरा रहेगा। हालांकि कहीं-कहीं हल्की बारिश या बूंदाबांदी होने की संभावना जताई गई है, लेकिन व्यापक या तेज बारिश के आसार फिलहाल कम हैं। लेकिन भीषण गर्मी और उमस से इस पूरे सप्ताह काफी राहत मिलने की उम्मीद है।
मौसम विभाग के अनुसार गुरुवार 24 जुलाई को अधिकतम तापमान 35 डिग्री सेल्सियस और न्यूनतम 25 डिग्री सेल्सियस रहने का अनुमान है। इस दिन आकाश सामान्यतः बादलों से घिरा रहेगा तथा हल्की बारिश हो सकती है। अधिकतम आर्द्रता (ह्यूमिडिटी) 80 प्रतिशत और न्यूनतम 60 प्रतिशत रहने की संभावना है। इस दिन के लिए किसी भी प्रकार की मौसम चेतावनी जारी नहीं की गई है। 25 जुलाई को अधिकतम तापमान 36 डिग्री तथा न्यूनतम 24 डिग्री सेल्सियस रहने का पूर्वानुमान है।
मौसम विभाग ने सामान्यतः बादल छाए रहने के साथ हल्की बारिश या बूंदाबांदी की संभावना जताई है। 26 जुलाई को अधिकतम तापमान 37 डिग्री और न्यूनतम 25 डिग्री सेल्सियस रहने की संभावना है। दिनभर आसमान में बादल छाए रहेंगे, जबकि आर्द्रता 70 से 60 प्रतिशत के बीच रह सकती है। इस दिन भी मौसम विभाग ने किसी प्रकार की चेतावनी जारी नहीं की है। 27 जुलाई को अधिकतम तापमान 36 डिग्री तथा न्यूनतम 26 डिग्री सेल्सियस रहने का अनुमान है।
मौसम विभाग के अनुसार दिनभर सामान्यतः बादल छाए रहेंगे और हल्की बारिश या बूंदाबांदी हो सकती है। 28 जुलाई को अधिकतम तापमान 36 डिग्री और न्यूनतम 25 डिग्री सेल्सियस रहने की संभावना है। पूरे दिन बादल छाए रहेंगे तथा हल्की बारिश हो सकती है। वहीं 29 जुलाई को अधिकतम तापमान में हल्की गिरावट दर्ज होने की संभावना है। इस दिन अधिकतम तापमान 34 डिग्री और न्यूनतम 24 डिग्री सेल्सियस रहने का अनुमान है। आसमान सामान्यतः बादलों से ढका रहेगा तथा आर्द्रता 80 से 70 प्रतिशत के बीच बनी रह सकती है। इस दिन भी किसी प्रकार का मौसम अलर्ट जारी नहीं किया गया है।
मौसम विभाग का कहना है कि फिलहाल मानसूनी गतिविधियां कमजोर बनी हुई हैं, इसलिए एनसीआर में तेज या लगातार बारिश की संभावना कम है। हालांकि दिनभर बादलों की आवाजाही बनी रहेगी और कई स्थानों पर हल्की बारिश या फुहारें पड़ सकती हैं। विशेषज्ञों के अनुसार बादलों की मौजूदगी और बीच-बीच में होने वाली हल्की बारिश के कारण तापमान नियंत्रित रहेगा, जिससे पिछले दिनों की तुलना में गर्मी और उमस से काफी राहत मिलेगी। कुल मिलाकर आने वाला सप्ताह एनसीआर के लोगों के लिए अपेक्षाकृत आरामदायक रहने वाला है। हालांकि बारिश अधिक नहीं होगी, लेकिन बादलों का घेरा और हल्की फुहारें मौसम को सुहावना बनाए रखेंगी।
--आईएएनएस
पीकेटी/पीएम
अस्वीकरण: यह न्यूज़ ऑटो फ़ीड्स द्वारा स्वतः प्रकाशित हुई खबर है। इस न्यूज़ में BhaskarHindi.com टीम के द्वारा किसी भी तरह का कोई बदलाव या परिवर्तन (एडिटिंग) नहीं किया गया है| इस न्यूज की एवं न्यूज में उपयोग में ली गई सामग्रियों की सम्पूर्ण जवाबदारी केवल और केवल न्यूज़ एजेंसी की है एवं इस न्यूज में दी गई जानकारी का उपयोग करने से पहले संबंधित क्षेत्र के विशेषज्ञों (वकील / इंजीनियर / ज्योतिष / वास्तुशास्त्री / डॉक्टर / न्यूज़ एजेंसी / अन्य विषय एक्सपर्ट) की सलाह जरूर लें। अतः संबंधित खबर एवं उपयोग में लिए गए टेक्स्ट मैटर, फोटो, विडियो एवं ऑडिओ को लेकर BhaskarHindi.com न्यूज पोर्टल की कोई भी जिम्मेदारी नहीं है|

title: Heat health alerts issued in parts of UK as rain returns for some this weekend - BBC
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
- Published
Yellow heat health alerts came into effect across central and southern England on Friday morning with temperatures forecast to reach 30C (86F) in places.
The alerts, issued by the UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA), cover the East and West Midlands, eastern England, London, south-east England and south-west England and are valid from 09:00 BST on Friday until 09:00 on Sunday.
The UKHSA warns that heat is expected to cause minor impacts for health and social care services, with an increased risk to life for vulnerable people.
It comes as parts of the UK prepare to see much-needed rain this weekend - although amounts are expected to be relatively small, especially in the south.
A drought was officially declared for large parts of north and mid-Wales on Thursday.
The rest of Wales, along with much of central, southern and eastern England, is in "prolonged dry weather" category - one stage below a drought.
Hosepipe bans are in force for millions of customers.
A surge of heat on Friday
North-westerly winds that have developed over recent days will briefly be cut off in southern areas on Friday, allowing temperatures to climb.
Central, southern and eastern parts of England will widely reach highs of 25-28C (77-82F), with a few spots expected to reach 30C.
Parts of east Wales could also climb into the high 20s, with other locations in Wales and northern England forecast to see 20-25C (68-77F).
It will be rather warm in Northern Ireland and Scotland too with afternoon highs reaching 19-23C.
Most areas will see sunny spells although thick cloud will linger in north-west Scotland with patchy rain arriving during Friday afternoon.
Temperatures will slowly drop over the weekend as fresher north-westerly winds return.
By Sunday afternoon highs will range from 16C (61F) in northern Scotland to 25C (77F) in south-east England.
It is expected to bring an end to the heatwave conditions that have persisted for 19 days so far at Usk in Monmouthshire and Exeter in Devon.
A little rain this weekend - but not enough
High pressure will retreat to the west allowing weather fronts to move south-eastwards across the UK which will bring some outbreaks of rain over the weekend.
Rainfall amounts could be reasonably significant over high ground in the north and the west of the UK, with 20mm (0.8in) possible in parts of north-west Scotland.
However, the wet weather is expected to be much patchier further south and east with large swathes of England and Wales forecast to see just a few millimetres at most.
It is likely that locations in the south will see little rain, if any.
Wisley in Surrey has now gone well over a month without any wet weather and it is by no means certain that it will record any rainfall on Friday or this weekend.
Either way, it is unlikely to bring much of an improvement for the large parts of England and Wales that are officially in drought - or just one step away - as a result of prolonged dry spells during spring and summer so far.
Will there be another heatwave next week?
Computer models are hinting at big contrasts in weather fortunes across different areas of the UK next week.
Weather fronts are expected to bring spells of rain in the north and west, with parts of Scotland, Northern Ireland and north-west England likely to see their most significant rainfall in a number of weeks.
However, high pressure may build to the south-east which would cause those weather fronts to stall.
That could allow dry and hot weather to rebuild across many parts of England and Wales, with temperatures potentially climbing back into the 30s Celsius in places.
There is the chance that heatwave conditions could return.
The details of the longer-range forecast do look rather uncertain so it is worth keeping up to date with full local forecasts on the BBC Weather website and app.

title: Heatwave and online shopping drive up retail sales in Great Britain - The Guardian
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
Online sales reached their highest share of retail spending in five years last month as record heat fuelled demand for air conditioners, outdoor products and clothing.
The Office for National Statistics (ONS) said the volume of retail sales in Great Britain rose 1% month on month in June, beating analysts’ expectations of a 0.3% decline, helped by hot weather and World Cup spending.
Heatwave conditions pushed year-on-year sales up 4.2% in June, almost double analysts’ expectations of 2.3%.
Hannah Finselbach, a senior statistician at the ONS, said: “With internet retailers faring well, the proportion of online sales has risen to its highest since spring 2021, when coronavirus restrictions were ending.”
After a revised 0.7% monthly fall in sales in April, two months of hot weather helped drive overall retail sales growth of 0.6% in the three months to the end of June, compared with the previous quarter.
Non-store retailers, primarily online businesses, enjoyed another bumper month, with sales soaring 4.4% in June, although down on the 6.1% growth recorded the previous month and 3.8% quarter on quarter.
Clothing and footwear retailers also had a strong month, with sales rising 1.9% from May. Clothing store sales increased 1.9% month on month, the biggest rise since September.
Sales at other non-food stores – retailers that are not clothing, household goods or department stores – rose 1.8% month on month, driven by computer and telecoms retailers.
Department stores, however, reported a 1.7% monthly decline in sales afer a 2.5% rise in May, when shoppers bought fans and paddling pools during the hot weather. Household goods retailers also reported weaker sales, down 0.6% month on month.
Samuel Edwards, the head of client portfolio management at the financial services firm Ebury, said: “Retailers scored an early summer goal in June, with retail sales outperforming expectations as warm weather, promotions and World Cup fever helped unlock consumer spending.”
While the so-called Burnham bounce and World Cup merriment had helped consumer confidence levels jump by the biggest margin for almost three years this month, Edwards warned that retailers still faced potential headwinds.
He said the higher energy price cap, persistently elevated interest rates and the inflationary pressures stemming from renewed tension in the Middle East could dent consumer confidence.
Melissa Minkow, the global director of retail strategy at CI&T, said: “Warm weather did a lot of the work for retailers in June. July’s figures will be an interesting watch, because we’ll see a response to Burnham’s cost of living policies come into play.
“Consumers tend to either pull back if they expect new policies to limit their funds or spend more if they think they’ll be good for their wallets.”

title: Heatwave anxiety can make you feel 'incredibly helpless' - here's how to manage it - BBC
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
Heatwave anxiety can make you feel 'incredibly helpless' - here's how to manage it
At the peak of the June heatwave, the warmest June on record in England, all Tavia Watts could think about was how hot it was in her two- and five-year-old's bedroom - above 30C in some cases.
"During the heatwave, I've got a high level of anxiety all the time and I'm pretty much useless," she told the BBC. "I've had to leave bedtime when my husband reads [my kids] stories to have a little cry and to calm down, because it's just too hot in their room and I'm afraid for them."
Feeling "incredibly helpless", the 36-year-old posted a short reel to her hundreds of followers on Instagram, in which she voiced her inner anxieties. To her surprise, it got more than 18,000 likes and 1,700 reposts.
In the video Tavia, a stay-at-home mum living in Cheltenham, says the heat "feels like the world is literally ending", she's "terrified for [her] children's lives", and that she "wants to scream at the people not taking this seriously".
Allow Instagram content?
Young people and women most at risk
Watts is not alone. A 2022 study from the University of Bath estimates that about 4.6% of people in the UK have climate anxiety, which is recognised as a form of mental distress by professional bodies and the NHS. Young people and women are among the groups most at risk, according to a study published in 2025.
"Anticipatory anxiety", related to heatwaves in particular, has become more common, say psychologists Dr Caroline Hickman from the University of Bath and Dr Emma Lawrance from the University of Oxford.
"Once people have been through a traumatic event like a flood or a heatwave, they anticipate the next one," Hickman, who offers therapy to individuals experiencing climate anxiety, told the BBC.
In the UK, "now we're getting the repeat, repeat, repeat [heatwaves]. It triggers that sense that 'I can't be safe in the world, and the world can't be trusted'," she added.
In June's heatwave, Hickman said she "had people saying they're going to kill themselves, I've had people saying they're going to terminate pregnancies, I've had people saying that they want to sell their houses and move somewhere cooler".
Watts said that her video "clearly struck a chord with people" - including therapists posting comments about coping mechanisms - and that she wanted to help others deal with climate anxiety.
"It can go one of two ways. You can either just fall into doomism and think, 'What is the point?'" she said.
"Or you can try to do something. I sign petitions during a heatwave; I organise climate community events; I run a free children's clothes swap shop. During the last heatwave, I finally became vegetarian, because then I can tell my kids at least I tried."
'The future is really scary'
Lizzie Ellen was "terrified" when she first learned about climate change in school, but at the time felt like it was "quite far off".
Now, the scorching temperatures of recent weeks have left the 38-year-old from west London "obsessively" reading about the heatwave and constantly checking her weather app.
Ellen, who gave birth to her first child a few months ago, told the BBC she had felt both isolated and worried about keeping her baby cool.
"With the baby, I've been staying indoors more, keeping on top of checking the temperature… so there's that isolation," she said.
"Then there's the future, which is really scary to be honest - thinking about how the heat we experienced back in June could become a daily reality within a very short time, [my baby's] lifetime… and that's pretty miserable, staying indoors all day in the dark."
Some have made major life changes in response to their climate concerns.
Ecologist Dr Francis Windram told the BBC that he had moved from Ascot, near London, to Lancaster in the north-west of England, specifically because of the cooler climate.
He still bought a portable air conditioning unit in spring as a precaution, and is concerned about his grandparents and his father living further south in Hertfordshire.
"We're looking at 35C-plus heat in houses that were not designed for it, with people who are not as able to regulate their temperature, and can't necessarily afford to just fit an AC," Windram said.
Lawrance had some recommendations on how to cope with climate anxiety:
Talk to others: There's often "a huge perception gap between how concerned people are about climate change, and how much they think other people care", said Lawrance - meaning you might wrongly assume that you're in the minority, or even that you're alone.
"Make space to be kind to yourself, to tend to those emotions and know that it's coming from a place of care for the world," she added.

It reported that the figure was obtained from the maximum-reading thermometer inside the monitoring enclosure, as shown in the attached video.
The Ministry said this reading—recorded in accordance with the technical standards adopted by meteorological stations—confirms that Azizia remains one of the areas in Libya most susceptible to extreme heat, particularly during intense heatwaves.
These current readings, it added, highlight Azizia's exceptional climatic nature and underscore the need to view historical climate records through a scientific lens; the area has long been known for recording extremely high temperatures when atmospheric conditions align.
Azizia'a 1922 world record temperature invalidated

full_text:
Heatwave exposes North Africa's fragile power grids
TUNIS – Major power outages sweeping Libya, Algeria and Tunisia are exposing deep structural weaknesses in North Africa's electricity networks, with extreme summer temperatures pushing ageing infrastructure beyond its limits despite the region's vast oil and gas wealth, Bloomberg reported.
Temperatures exceeding 40 degrees Celsius have driven electricity demand sharply higher across the region, forcing utilities to impose rolling blackouts while struggling to maintain power stations and transmission networks that have suffered years of underinvestment.
The disruptions underline a striking contradiction for a region that holds some of Africa's largest hydrocarbon reserves yet continues to face recurring electricity shortages because of ageing grids, fuel supply constraints and financial difficulties.
Libya suffered the most severe disruption after a transmission line failure triggered a nationwide blackout on Friday, shutting down major power stations and interrupting water supplies.
According to Libyan officials, authorities turned to neighbouring Egypt for emergency assistance, activating a cross-border electricity interconnection that enabled power stations to begin restarting. Egypt subsequently started supplying around 70 megawatts of electricity while repair work continued.
Officials have also discussed reducing natural gas exports to Italy temporarily in order to prioritise domestic electricity generation during the peak summer season, although no decision has been implemented.
Despite possessing some of Africa's largest crude oil reserves, Libya remains heavily dependent on imported fuels for electricity generation because of its limited refining capacity and infrastructure damaged by years of political turmoil and conflict.
The state electricity company had already warned last month of an unprecedented shortage of natural gas and heavy fuel oil, saying additional resources were urgently needed to avoid widespread outages.
Neighbouring Algeria, one of Europe's most important gas suppliers, also experienced its worst blackout in roughly two decades after a technical failure at a power plant south of Algiers disrupted electricity supplies across about a quarter of the country's provinces.
The outage quickly spilled across the border into Tunisia, whose electricity system depends heavily on Algerian gas and power imports.
Tunisia's state utility STEG blamed a combination of the regional heatwave, soaring electricity demand and the Algerian disruption for repeated outages before announcing scheduled electricity cuts lasting up to seven hours a day in dozens of towns and cities, including the capital Tunis.
The company urged households to limit air-conditioning use, saying the emergency measures were necessary to preserve the stability of the national electricity network.
The blackouts have triggered mounting public frustration and criticism from businesses, particularly hotels, retailers and food producers already grappling with sluggish economic growth and rising operating costs.
Lawmakers have called for an emergency parliamentary session to examine the causes of the crisis and accelerate investment in the country's struggling electricity sector.
Unlike recent disruptions to global energy markets caused by tensions involving Iran, analysts say the latest outages primarily reflect domestic infrastructure weaknesses rather than international supply shocks.
Egypt, which endured months of scheduled blackouts in 2023 amid gas shortages, has so far avoided similar disruptions this summer after securing additional liquefied natural gas cargoes to strengthen domestic supplies.

title: Brits dodge the high street as heatwave boosts online shopping - City AM

full_text:
Retail sales jumped in June as the heatwave and heavy discounting drove Brits towards online shopping.
The volume of retail sales jumped by one per cent in June, having slowed slightly from 1.2 per cent growth in May, according to the Office for National Statistics (ONS).
Online shopping grew to the largest proportion of total retail sales since spring 2021, when the covid-19 pandemic kept Brits away from the high street.
Non-store retailing saw the biggest jump among spending sectors in June, rising 4.4 per cent, while department store sale volumes slipped by 1.7 per cent.
Sales at food stores inched up by 0.3 per cent last month, as purchases at household goods stores fell by 0.6 per cent.
Hannah Finselbach, senior statistician at the ONS, said: “Internet retailers did especially well, with business telling us that this was because of promotions and the warm weather.”
Online retailers saw huge demand for outdoor products, air conditioning and clothing, Finselbach said.
Fuel sales slip after stockpiling ends
While high demand for fuel had buoyed retail sales figures in recent months, demand for petrol and diesel has appeared to fall off a cliff in the last quarter.
Automotive fuel sales fell by 0.8 per cent in June, marking a six per cent drop over the last three months.
While motorists had been stockpiling fuel in the first few weeks of the Iran war, soaring energy prices in April kept motorists away from the pumps and demand has yet to return, the ONS said.
Inflation took a surprise drop from 2.8 to 2.6 per cent in June, dragged down by the sharp fall in fuel prices which came a few weeks ago.
But energy prices are tipped to rise again following fresh strikes between the US and Iran, including Houthi attacks on tankers in the Red Sea.
Retailers will be hoping that the heatwave and World Cup will lift sales in July too, as the higher traffic to pubs is likely to have knock-on effects on the wider high street.
Inflation could hit consumer spending
But Thomas Pugh, chief economist at RSM, warned that recent rises in oil prices could weigh on consumer spending. He said: “If those prices persist through the summer, inflation is likely to rise above 3.5 per cent, increasing the risk that the Bank of England raises interest rates.
“That would squeeze household budgets and could start to weigh on consumer spending.”
The British Retail Consortium (BRC), which represents the UK’s leading retailers, has called on Andy Burnham to cut so-called “policy costs” on energy bills for high street businesses.
Harvir Dhillon, the trade body’s chief economist, said: “To support growth and keep inflation under control, Government should address the taxes and levies that increase businesses’ energy bills.
“Otherwise, rising costs will continue to constrain investment and make it harder for retailers to keep prices low for customers in the future.”

title: Heatwave 2026: Forget face fans and air-con... I need a bedroom mosquito net - echo live
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
Sure, the winter will be along soon enough, we say - and the evenings are already starting to shorten. Then again, we do love a good gripe too, and the heatwave has offered up plenty of opportunity on that front.
I get it, and I can understand why people may be in the market for an air-con if these long, hot summers keep on coming.
At the sweltering Marquee the other night, I sat next to a couple with face fans and envied their cool-headed forward-planning.
But I have another bugbear altogether that has arrived this summer, and one I have never noticed before.
Lads, I am being hounded by biting mosquitoes every night, so much so that I am seriously considering purchasing a heretofore exotic item we associate only with countries on the equator - a mosquito net for the bed. (I note they are as cheap as €12.99 on Amazon).
It all started at the beginning of the first heat dome, when I was awoken at dawn by a high-pitched whine fluttering around my head. It was the sound of a blood-hungry mozzie beating its wings several hundred times a second, seeking out its next meal.
They got in the next night, too, and we itched and scratched for days until we realised the only way to combat them was to close the windows before dusk, then open them again when all the lights were out. Even then, a few snuck in to grab a tasty snack.
Out in the country, we’re used to midges, which hunt in packs outdoors and tend to congregate above hedges as night falls. I’ve also suffered a personal vendetta from a horse fly who dines on my blood every summer when I am out on my daily walk.
These are the slings and arrows of a normal summer - but the introduction of mosquitoes is slightly more worrying. It’s not just their annoying whine and bite, it’s their association with nasty diseases in the public imagination.
Malaria, which can be spread by mosquitoes, is one of the oldest known diseases, and has been a threat in Ireland intermittently down the centuries.
One archaeologist even linked early Bronze Age Irish people to the disease - tests on a cemetery site revealed that their bodies had widespread iron-anaemia deficiency associated with malaria victims.
However, global warming may well see the return of malaria to Ireland in the future - and here in Cork, we are particularly vulnerable.
After all, Cork city was built on marshland, while we have plenty of rivers such as the Lee, the Blackwater, and the Bandon where such insects may flourish.
For my own part, our home overlooks a floodplain of the Lee controlled by the two dams, and since April, it has been unusually high - perhaps creating a better environment for the mosquitoes which have plagued us this summer.
Cork is also milder and damper than most counties, and we know historically that malaria has been a problem associated with the county. It wasn’t given the nickname of ‘Cork Fever’ for nothing in olden times.
The most profile victim of it in previous centuries was someone who will get little sympathy in these parts: Oliver Cromwell.
It is thought he contacted malaria while based in Youghal during his ruthless army campaign in Munster in 1649. The story goes he refused to take quinine, the only effective anti-malarial drug known at the time, because it was ‘the Jesuit powder’.Oh dear, never mind.
Cromwell died nine years later from complications of the recurring malaria, at the time referred to as a ‘tertian ague’ or ‘bastard tertian ague’ in some quarters. It is a phrase I often feel like using when one whines above my head in bed.
Two centuries after Cromwell, in the 1850s, soldiers returning from the Crimean War brought plasmodium, the parasite causing malaria, to Cork’s mosquito population. Almost 1,000 cases of the disease had been reported by the time the outbreak ended a decade later.
We sill have a dozen or so species of mosquito in Ireland, but none which at present cause malaria or other illnesses spread by them, such as dengue fever, zika, and chikungunya.
We can only hope that situation doesn’t change, although the news that mosquitoes had made it to Iceland recently makes you fear where this is heading. It’s not known how they got there but one theory is that they hitched a ride on a plane.
Cases of malaria have occurred near airports in the UK, and blamed on such stowaways.
This has been highlighted in France, where there were more cases of mosquito-borne diseases in 2022 than the previous 20 years combined.
The current spell of hot weather accelerated the mosquito breeding cycle, meaning larger numbers of the pests could emerge, many of them in my bedroom. The females, needing blood to produce their frequent eggs, thus bite even more often.
Experts are keeping a close eye on the arrival of invasive species, such as the Asian tiger mosquito, whose eggs have been detected in the UK.
Professor Gerry Killeen, who holds the AXA Research Chair in Applied Pathogen Ecology at UCC, and spent almost two decades living in east Africa, studying the behavioural ecology of mosquitoes, was asked if the Asian tiger mosquito could come to Ireland in a radio interview last summer.
“It wouldn’t surprise me,” he replied. “One of the things that will turn these particular mosquitoes into a global problem is that their eggs are very robust.”
“A lot of these diseases are considered primarily tropical diseases, but, of course, that’s changing as the climate changes,” said Prof Killeen.
As for advice on how to combat mozzies, the experts say mosquito repellent is number one,
Covering up at dawn and in the evenings is also good practice, as mosquitoes are most prevalent at these times. Wearing colourful clothes and perfumes can also attract the insects, so opt for long garments and fragrance-free products.
Avoid areas with stagnant water - even something as small as a flower pot will attract mosquitoes - and try to seek out colder rooms as the insects favour heat.

While a few showers/storms are possible north in the morning, there is another chance for severe storm potential in the afternoon and evening as a cold front moves through our warm and humid atmosphere. However, the risk is conditional. There will be a fairly strong “cap” (warm air aloft) that could inhibit storm development. If the cap can be overcome, severe hazards would include golf ball-sized hail and damaging wind gusts 60-70 mph. There is a low, but possible, risk of a tornado from the James River Valley to Devils Lake basin and up through northern MN. That’s the same area that the SPC has a Level 2 risk for scattered severe storms.
FIRST ALERT SATURDAY - SUNDAY: A hot weekend expected with potential for extreme heat and heat-related impacts. We will have temperatures Saturday and Sunday soaring into the 90s (some near 100) with a good deal of sunshine and wind coming in from the south. Sunday will be several degrees hotter than Saturday. Humid conditions will also be on the increase into the weekend, causing heat indices to soar. There is a ‘major’ risk of excessive heat for areas mainly south of Hwy 2 on Saturday, and most of the region Sunday as heat-related impacts rise. This means that the heat affects anyone without cooling/hydration as well as health systems and industries. For these reasons, we have raised First Alert Weather Days for Saturday and Sunday. A ‘Major’ risk of excessive heat, according to the NWS, means:
- Heat of this type represents a major risk to all individuals who are:
- Dangerous to anyone without proper hydration or adequate cooling
- For those without air conditioning, living spaces can become deadly during the afternoon and evening. Fans and open windows will not be as effective.
- Poor air quality is possible
- Power interruptions may occur as electrical demands increase
NEXT WEEK: The heat lingers into the start of the week with temperatures Monday still expected to be in the 90s for many, along with clear skies. Heat-related impacts linger in a few areas. Tuesday remains toasty in the low-to-mid 90s. For the rest of the week, expect continued above-average highs in the upper-80s and low-90s. Most will remain rain-free through the week as well.
FARGO 7-DAY FORECAST

FIRST ALERT Sunday: Very hot and sunny. Heat index 105+. Low: 71 High: 99
Monday: Hot and sunny. Low: 72 High: 95
Tuesday: Hot and sunny. Low: 67 High: 95

Thursday: Sunny with continued heat. Low: 67 High: 91

title: Are you making these summer health mistakes? Doctors debunk common summer misconceptions - Gulf News
search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
Experts reveal the myths that could put your health at risk this UAE summer
Dubai: You wait until you're thirsty before drinking water. You assume staying indoors is enough to beat the heat. You switch on a fan and think you're protected. They may sound like harmless summer habits, but doctors say some of the most common advice people follow during the UAE's hottest months is based on myths, not medicine.
While many of these beliefs have been passed down through families or amplified on social media, experts warn they can increase the risk of dehydration, heat exhaustion, and even life-threatening heat stroke.
Here's what doctors say UAE residents should stop believing and what they should do instead.
If there's one misconception Dr. Rayan Mohammed, general practitioner at Valeo Health, wants people to leave behind this summer, it's waiting for thirst before reaching for a glass of water.
“In reality, thirst is a late indicator of dehydration, particularly in older adults, children, and people spending prolonged periods in air-conditioned environments where fluid loss is less noticeable,” Mohammed told Gulf News.
Another myth she often hears is that drinking ice-cold water in extreme heat is harmful.
“There is no medical evidence to support this. Cold water is perfectly safe for most people and can actually make hydration more comfortable, encouraging adequate fluid intake.”
Mohammed has also cautioned against relying on sunscreen alone.
“Sunscreen prevents UV damage and sunburn, but it does not prevent heat exhaustion or heat stroke.”
For those who develop heat exhaustion, she has stressed that the response should be immediate, not after “five more minutes.”
People should stop all activity, move into air conditioning or deep shade, drink cool water with electrolytes, cool the skin, and monitor symptoms. If they don't improve within 30 minutes, or confusion, unconsciousness, or the absence of sweating develops, emergency medical care is needed.
Feeling a fan blowing across your face may offer relief, but Wayne Martini Jr., MD, emergency medicine physician at Mayo Clinic, has bared that it can create a false sense of security during extreme heat.
“Once the air is hotter than your skin, roughly above 35°C, a fan can blow hot air over you and add heat rather than remove it,” explained Martini Jr.

title: Heat illness risk remains despite cooler temperatures, ER doctor warns - KSLA
search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
TEXARKANA (KSLA) - A cooler breeze moved through the area Thursday, but doctors say the threat of heat-related illness has not gone away.
Dr. Eric Sass, an emergency room physician at Christus St. Michael Health System in Texarkana, said the combination of temperature and humidity — known as the heat index — is what drives the body’s core temperature up.
“It is not just the temperature, it is also the humidity. It is the combination of what is called the heat index that ends up causing people’s temperature to rise and their ability to sweat,” Sass said.
Patients arriving with heat exhaustion, heat stroke
Sass said the hospital has seen a number of patients come in with heat exhaustion and heat strokes over the last several weeks. He said symptoms include chills, nausea and an intense urge to drink fluids.
“The symptoms — they occur on top of sweating — will be chills and nausea, and they end up wanting to drink and drink and drink,” Sass said.
Who is most at risk
According to Sass, those at greatest risk include outdoor workers, children who play outside, and elderly individuals who may be living alone. He said heat illness can come on quickly.
“By the time somebody figures out they are extremely thirsty and they are trying to keep up, they are really behind on their fluids inside of their body,” Sass said.
Walton Dee, who works for a lawn service, said conditions remain a concern even on cooler days.
“It’s cooler today, but it’s still hot out here,” Dee said.
Sass said people should keep water and electrolyte drinks on hand and not hesitate to visit the emergency room if needed.

full_text:
Heavy rain inundates Rajasthan; red alert in two districts, train services hit
Heavy rain inundates Rajasthan; red alert in two districts, train services hit
Jaipur, Heavy rainfall lashed several parts of Rajasthan on Friday, with Pachpadra in Balotra district and Amet in Rajsamand recording the highest rainfall of 9 cm each during the past 24 hours, officials said.
The India Meteorological Department issued a red alert for Banswara and Dungarpur districts, an orange alert for nine districts and a yellow warning for the remaining districts on Friday.
Rail services on the Mumbai route were affected after rising water levels near Bridge No. 203 between Gholvad and Umargam Road stations on the Western Railway led to the suspension of train movement.
The North Western Railway said three trains scheduled for July 24 Bandra Terminus-Jaisalmer, Dadar-Bhagat Ki Kothi and Bandra Terminus-Ajmer were cancelled due to the disruption.
According to the centre, moderate to heavy rainfall was recorded at several places in eastern Rajasthan during the 24 hours ending Friday morning, while many areas in western Rajasthan also received moderate to heavy showers accompanied by thunderstorms.
Pachpadra and Amet received the highest rainfall of 9 cm each while Salumbar, Jhadol in Udaipur and Badesar in Chittorgarh recorded 7 cm of rainfall each. Several other places received less than 7 cm of rainfall.
Heavy rain affected normal life in several districts, inundating agricultural fields in Hanumangarh, Sri Ganganagar, Beawar, Bhilwara and Udaipur. The Som and Mahi rivers are flowing in spate in Dungarpur, while waterlogging, overflowing seasonal rivers and damage to culverts disrupted road connectivity to several villages and small towns.
The IMD has forecast heavy to very heavy rainfall in Banswara and Dungarpur and issued an orange alert for Bhilwara, Chittorgarh, Pratapgarh, Rajsamand, Salumbar, Sirohi, Udaipur, Balotra and Barmer districts, while yellow alerts remain in force for several other parts of the state.
The state's highest maximum temperature was recorded at 38.4 degrees Celsius in Phalodi on Thursday, while the minimum temperature settled at 23.4 degrees Celsius in Dabok .

full_text:
Pune's Tamhini Ghat records 280 mm rain as IMD issues red alert
Pune district's ghat sections received another spell of torrential monsoon rain, with Tamhini Ghat recording 280 mm in 24 hours ending Thursday morning. The IMD has issued a red alert for the region, while Pune city witnessed light to moderate showers. The district has already surpassed its 12-year July rainfall record.
Tamhini ghat, which received 280 mm of rainfall in 24 hours ending at 8.30 am on Thursday. Pic/X/@meet_abhijit
Pune district’s ghat sections witnessed another spell of over 200 mm torrential monsoon rain in 24 hours ending at 8.30 am on Thursday. The Tamhini ghat recorded the highest rainfall during this period, 280 mm, prompting the India Meteorological Department (IMD) to issue a red alert for the region.
While the hill ranges continued to receive heavy to very heavy rainfall, Pune city and the adjoining plains experienced only light to moderate showers accompanied by gusty morning winds. The latest spell comes even as Pune has already surpassed its 12-year July rainfall record, with more rain forecast over the next four days.

title: China renews orange alert as Typhoon Noul approaches the southern coast - China Daily
search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
China renews orange alert as Typhoon Noul approaches the southern coast
China's National Meteorological Center renewed an orange alert for Typhoon Noul on Friday, warning that the storm is expected to strengthen into a typhoon before making landfall along the southern coast between Saturday night and early Sunday.
At 8 am on Friday, Noul, the 12th named storm of the year, was centered about 505 kilometers northeast of Manila in the Philippines, packing maximum sustained winds of 20 meters per second. Its central pressure was 995 hectopascals.
The storm is forecast to move west-northwest while steadily intensifying. It is expected to enter the South China Sea late Friday or early Saturday before approaching the coasts of Guangdong and Fujian provinces.
Forecasters said Noul is likely to make landfall between Zhuhai in Guangdong and Zhangpu county in Fujian between Saturday evening and early Sunday, with maximum sustained winds of 33 to 40 meters per second.
After landfall, the storm is expected to turn northward and gradually weaken as it moves inland.
Strong winds are forecast across the northeastern South China Sea, the Bashi Channel, the waters east of Taiwan, the Taiwan Strait, the southern East China Sea and coastal areas of Taiwan, Fujian, and Guangdong from Friday afternoon through Saturday afternoon.
Most of those areas are expected to experience sustained winds of at least 10.8 meters per second. Areas near the storm's center may experience winds of up to 41.4 meters per second.
Heavy rain is also forecast for parts of Taiwan from Friday afternoon to Saturday afternoon, with some areas expected to receive 100 to 180 millimeters of rainfall.
Local authorities are urged to activate emergency response measures and suspend large outdoor gatherings and hazardous outdoor operations. Ships operating in the affected waters are required to return to port or seek shelter.
Residents were advised to remain indoors, secure loose outdoor structures, and stay alert even if winds temporarily weaken when the eye of the storm passes, as dangerous conditions can quickly return.
Authorities also warned of the risk of flash floods and geological disasters triggered by heavy rainfall in affected areas.

Red Alert Construction is scheduled in Richardson this weekend affecting commuters that use 75 during DART bridge arch installation.
On Thursday July 23rd on southbound 75 between Arapaho and Spring Valley Road alternating lanes will be closed overnight from 9 pm - 5 am.
On Friday, July 24th that's when the RED ALERT road work starts with both northbound and southbound 75 being completely shut down between Belt Line Road and Arapaho Road from 9 pm - 6 am, traffic will be diverted to the service road.
On Saturday, July 25th just northbound 75 will be closed between Belt Line Road and Arapaho Road from 9 pm to 8 am, again traffic will be diverted to the service road.
Also DART Silver Line rail service between Plano’s Shiloh Road Station and UTD Station will also be suspended overnight from Friday, July 24th through Sunday, July 26th as the trains cannot operate beneath the active construction zone while crews install a 400-foot decorative steel arch on the north side of the highway to match an arch on the south side was installed earlier this summer.
